fingerprint: validation:the common-dir lock fix covers three targets (refs.lock, packed-refs.lock, and the loose-ref-lock directory walk under refs/), but the only mandated linked-worktree lock test covers packed-refs.lock — a simple os.stat. the loose-ref-lock walkdir relocation, the most complex and error-prone part of the fix, has no mandated linked-worktree test.
message: The common-dir lock fix covers three targets (refs.lock, packed-refs.lock, and the loose-ref-lock directory walk under refs/), but the only mandated linked-worktree lock test covers packed-refs.lock — a simple os.Stat. The loose-ref-lock WalkDir relocation, the most complex and error-prone part of the fix, has no mandated linked-worktree test.
material_impact: A loose-ref-lock walk wrongly left pointing at the per-worktree git dir (filepath.Join(gitDir,'refs')) instead of commonDir would silently never detect shared loose-ref locks in a linked worktree, yet pass all mandated tests since the existing loose-ref-lock test runs in a primary repo where the two dirs coincide.
fix_direction: Add a mandated linked-worktree sub-test that places a loose ref lock (e.g. refs/heads/<branch>.lock) under the common dir and asserts executor_git_guard_inconclusive, mirroring the packed-refs.lock linked-worktree test.
uncertainty: The implementer may add such a test voluntarily; the contract simply does not require it.

fingerprint: quality:the goal narrative lists 'no stale lock files' among preconditions to 'keep every other precondition unchanged,' directly contradicting scope-in bullet 2 and acceptance criterion 6, which require changing the stale-lock precondition to resolve shared-ref locks against the git common dir.
message: The Goal narrative lists 'no stale lock files' among preconditions to 'KEEP every other precondition unchanged,' directly contradicting Scope-in bullet 2 and acceptance criterion 6, which require changing the stale-lock precondition to resolve shared-ref locks against the git common dir.
material_impact: An implementer prioritizing the Goal's 'keep unchanged' list over the detailed AC could under-deliver the common-dir resolution; in practice criterion 6's mandated tests catch this, so it is reconcilable rather than stuck.
fix_direction: Reword the Goal's keep-list to say the stale-lock precondition's detection behavior is preserved while its path resolution is corrected for linked worktrees, removing 'no stale lock files' from the verbatim 'unchanged' enumeration.

fingerprint: validation:the goal's headline regression (primary checkout blocked when a sibling worktree exists) and acceptance criterion 1 are not tied to any mandated test asserting gitguardprechecks returns ok=true in the primary worktree while a sibling exists.
message: The Goal's headline regression (primary checkout blocked when a sibling worktree exists) and acceptance criterion 1 are not tied to any mandated test asserting gitGuardPrechecks returns ok=true in the PRIMARY worktree while a sibling exists.
material_impact: The exact regression described in the Goal is fixed by the same single code removal as the linked-worktree case, so a missing dedicated test does not make the implementation wrong; it merely leaves the headline scenario unpinned.
fix_direction: Add an explicit assertion that gitGuardPrechecks(primaryRoot) returns ok=true with a non-nil snapshot after a sibling worktree has been created via git worktree add.

fingerprint: validation:acceptance criterion 6a claims its primary-worktree test 'exercises the relative-path resolution branch,' but the specified test only asserts the inconclusive outcome and cannot observe whether --git-common-dir returned a relative path or whether the filepath.join(root, commondir) branch actually executed.
message: Acceptance criterion 6a claims its primary-worktree test 'exercises the relative-path resolution branch,' but the specified test only asserts the inconclusive outcome and cannot observe whether --git-common-dir returned a relative path or whether the filepath.Join(root, commonDir) branch actually executed.
material_impact: On a git version where --git-common-dir returns an absolute path in a primary worktree, the IsAbs short-circuit runs and the relative-join branch is never exercised, yet the test still passes — giving false confidence that the relative-resolution branch is verified.
fix_direction: Either soften the criterion's claim to 'verifies inconclusive outcome under primary-worktree path resolution,' or have the test assert on the resolved common-dir path form (e.g., via a small testable helper) so the relative branch is observably exercised.

fingerprint: validation:acceptance criterion 9 (code comment must document points a–d) is purely prose with no machine-checkable outcome. no listed validation command verifies that a comment exists or covers the required shared-ref / restore-failure / concurrency-residual / common-dir-resolution points; make check does not assert comment content.
message: Acceptance criterion 9 (code comment must document points a–d) is purely prose with no machine-checkable outcome. No listed validation command verifies that a comment exists or covers the required shared-ref / restore-failure / concurrency-residual / common-dir-resolution points; make check does not assert comment content.
material_impact: Comment-content correctness can only be checked by human review, so an implementation could satisfy all runnable checks while omitting or misstating the documented residual — but this does not block or misdirect the code change itself.
fix_direction: Either accept this as human-reviewed (mark as a review-checklist item rather than an acceptance criterion), or keep it explicitly advisory so it is not treated as a gating, machine-verified outcome.

fingerprint: validation:criterion 5 asserts that sparse index and split index preconditions 'still return executor_git_guard_inconclusive', but no existing test covers sparse/split index and the contract requires none (criterion 7 mandates only the two stale-lock tests). that portion of the acceptance is not backed by a runnable check, so a regression in sparse/split detection would still pass `go test -run testgitguard` and `make check`.
message: Criterion 5 asserts that sparse index and split index preconditions 'still return executor_git_guard_inconclusive', but no existing test covers sparse/split index and the contract requires none (criterion 7 mandates only the two stale-lock tests). That portion of the acceptance is not backed by a runnable check, so a regression in sparse/split detection would still pass `go test -run TestGitGuard` and `make check`.
material_impact: The 'do not weaken' guarantee for sparse/split index is unverifiable via the stated validation commands, so an accidental regression there would not be caught by the contract's gates — but the targeted change (removing the multi-worktree precondition) does not touch this code path, so risk is low.
fix_direction: Optionally add a sparse-index and split-index inconclusive test to git_guard_test.go so the full criterion-5 list is machine-checked, or scope criterion 5's verifiable subset explicitly.
uncertainty: Confirmed that no sparse/split test exists today; whether the author intends regression coverage for them is unknown.

fingerprint: validation:criterion 1 (a clean primary worktree with a linked sibling no longer returns executor_git_guard_inconclusive) is not directly backed by a required test. the mandated tests run prechecks on the linked worktree (criteria 2/3) or on a primary repo with a stale lock (7a, no sibling); none runs prechecks on a primary worktree while a sibling worktree exists.
message: Criterion 1 (a clean PRIMARY worktree with a linked sibling no longer returns executor_git_guard_inconclusive) is not directly backed by a required test. The mandated tests run prechecks on the linked worktree (criteria 2/3) or on a primary repo with a stale lock (7a, no sibling); none runs prechecks on a primary worktree while a sibling worktree exists.
material_impact: The headline primary-worktree behavior is only transitively verified via the linked-worktree test (same removed code path), so a primary-specific regression would not be independently caught — but because both paths share the removed precondition, divergent failure is unlikely.
fix_direction: Add a small assertion that gitGuardPrechecks on the primary root returns ok=true after `git worktree add` creates a sibling, making criterion 1 independently verifiable.
uncertainty: The linked-worktree clean test exercises the same removed precondition, so the gap may be considered acceptable by the author.

fingerprint: validation:validation command 1 (`go test ./internal/app -run testgitguard`) is a regexp substring filter that only runs tests whose names contain 'testgitguard'. the contract mandates several new tests but does not require them to follow this naming convention, so a non-conforming test name would be silently skipped by command 1 (it would pass without exercising the new behavior).
message: Validation command 1 (`go test ./internal/app -run TestGitGuard`) is a regexp substring filter that only runs tests whose names contain 'TestGitGuard'. The contract mandates several new tests but does not require them to follow this naming convention, so a non-conforming test name would be silently skipped by command 1 (it would pass without exercising the new behavior).
material_impact: None in practice: `make check` runs `go test ./...` (Makefile:22-23,65), executing the full suite regardless of test name, so any new test runs and fails the gate on wrong output. The vacuity gap in command 1 is fully covered by command 2, and the existing file convention (every test is TestGitGuard*) makes a divergent name unlikely.
fix_direction: Optional: state in the contract that new tests should be named with a TestGitGuard prefix so command 1 also exercises them, or simply rely on `make check` as the authoritative full-suite gate.
uncertainty: This is mitigated by `make check` and the strong existing naming convention; included only for completeness, not as a real soundness failure.

fingerprint: scope:the goal narrative lists 'no stale lock files' among the preconditions to 'keep every other precondition unchanged', and scope.in item 1 says to modify the file 'while preserving all other gitguardprechecks behavior' — but scope.in item 2 and several acceptance criteria require changing the stale-lock precondition's path resolution (refs.lock, packed-refs.lock, and the refs/ walk now resolved against the git common dir). the high-level 'keep everything else unchanged' framing contradicts the explicit secondary stale-lock fix.
message: The Goal narrative lists 'no stale lock files' among the preconditions to 'KEEP every other precondition unchanged', and scope.in item 1 says to modify the file 'while preserving all other gitGuardPrechecks behavior' — but scope.in item 2 and several acceptance criteria require changing the stale-lock precondition's path resolution (refs.lock, packed-refs.lock, and the refs/ walk now resolved against the git common dir). The high-level 'keep everything else unchanged' framing contradicts the explicit secondary stale-lock fix.
material_impact: An implementer skimming the Goal could read 'no stale lock files ... unchanged' literally and implement only the multi-worktree removal, skipping the common-dir relocation of the ref-lock checks. This would leave the stale-lock precondition ineffective for shared ref locks in linked worktrees — the exact gap the contract intends to close.
fix_direction: Tighten the Goal/scope.in wording so they do not claim the stale-lock precondition is left unchanged. Either drop 'no stale lock files' from the Goal's keep-unchanged list, or qualify it as 'detection semantics unchanged; path resolution corrected to use the git common dir for shared ref locks', mirroring the acceptance-criteria language.
uncertainty: The acceptance criteria are detailed and test-gated (two dedicated stale-lock tests in the validation commands), which authoritatively resolves the ambiguity, so the practical risk of a wrong implementation is low. This is a coherence/wording inconsistency rather than a blocking defect.

fingerprint: correctness:the contract applies careful per-worktree-vs-common-dir analysis to lock files but does not surface or test the analogous assumption for the split-index precondition, which reads the per-worktree git dir (--absolute-git-dir) for sharedindex.* entries. it is left implicit that split-index detection via the per-worktree git dir remains correct in a linked worktree.
message: The contract applies careful per-worktree-vs-common-dir analysis to lock files but does not surface or test the analogous assumption for the split-index precondition, which reads the per-worktree git dir (--absolute-git-dir) for sharedindex.* entries. It is left implicit that split-index detection via the per-worktree git dir remains correct in a linked worktree.
material_impact: Removing the blanket multi-worktree bail newly routes split-index-in-a-linked-worktree repos through the split-index check. If git stores sharedindex.* in the common dir (not the per-worktree dir), the per-worktree ReadDir finds nothing, the precondition silently no-ops in linked worktrees, and the guard proceeds where the contract intends it to bail — silently weakening a precondition that scope-out forbids weakening.
fix_direction: Surface the assumption explicitly: state where git stores sharedindex.* relative to --absolute-git-dir vs --git-common-dir in linked worktrees, and either confirm the per-worktree dir is correct (and add a split-index-in-linked-worktree test) or extend the common-dir resolution to the split-index check as was done for shared-ref locks.
uncertainty: I could not empirically confirm whether git stores sharedindex.* in the per-worktree git dir or the common dir (the verification command was declined); if it is per-worktree, the current spec is correct and this is moot.

fingerprint: quality:relocating the loose-ref-lock walk entirely from the per-worktree git dir to the common dir silently assumes there are no per-worktree loose ref locks worth detecting (e.g., refs/bisect/*, refs/worktree/*), since the per-worktree refs/ tree would no longer be walked. this trade-off is not stated.
message: Relocating the loose-ref-lock walk entirely from the per-worktree git dir to the common dir silently assumes there are no per-worktree loose ref locks worth detecting (e.g., refs/bisect/*, refs/worktree/*), since the per-worktree refs/ tree would no longer be walked. This trade-off is not stated.
material_impact: A stale per-worktree loose ref lock (outside refs/heads, e.g. under refs/bisect or refs/worktree) would no longer be detected in a linked worktree; the residual is small because BISECT_LOG is covered by the marker check, but the assumption is undocumented.
fix_direction: Either state explicitly that per-worktree loose ref locks are out of scope (and why they are negligible), or have the walk cover both the common refs/ and the per-worktree refs/ directories.
uncertainty: Per-worktree loose ref locks are exotic in practice, so real-world impact is likely negligible.

fingerprint: validation:the primary-worktree stale-lock test is required to hand-construct the lock path as filepath.join(root, '.git', 'packed-refs.lock') to exercise the relative-path branch, but the contract elsewhere only hedges that --git-common-dir 'may be' relative. the test's correctness depends on the output being definitely relative '.git', a stronger claim than the surfaced assumption.
message: The primary-worktree stale-lock test is required to hand-construct the lock path as filepath.Join(root, '.git', 'packed-refs.lock') to exercise the relative-path branch, but the contract elsewhere only hedges that --git-common-dir 'may be' relative. The test's correctness depends on the output being definitely relative '.git', a stronger claim than the surfaced assumption.
material_impact: If a git build/config returned an absolute common dir for a primary repo, the hardcoded '.git' path would not match where the code looks (which would use the absolute path verbatim), and the primary test would fail to exercise the relative branch or fail outright — a brittle test rather than a wrong implementation.
fix_direction: Have the primary test resolve the lock path the same way the implementation does (read actual --git-common-dir output and apply the same conditional join), so the test tracks git's real behavior instead of asserting a specific output form.
uncertainty: Freshly git-init'd repos reliably report '.git', so the practical risk is low.

fingerprint: scope:the contract gives conflicting scope boundaries for git_guard.go: one section says to remove/replace only the multi-worktree precondition, while scope in later requires changing stale-lock-file path resolution.
message: The contract gives conflicting scope boundaries for git_guard.go: one section says to remove/replace only the multi-worktree precondition, while Scope in later requires changing stale-lock-file path resolution.
material_impact: An implementer following the earlier 'only' restriction could omit the stale-lock common-dir fix, causing the implementation to fail later acceptance criteria; an implementer following the later Scope in could appear to violate the earlier scope limit.
fix_direction: Revise the earlier scope sentence to explicitly allow both changes in git_guard.go: removing the multi-worktree inconclusive precondition and updating stale shared-ref lock resolution to use the git common dir.
